coredata.dc:title: Analytical methodology for the electro-catalytic determination of estradiol and progesterone based on graphene quantum dots and poly(sulfosalicylic acid) co-modified electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Arvand, Majid
Hemmati, Shiva
coredata.dc:description: The goal of this study was to develop an electroanalytical method for the simultaneous determination of steroid hormones for the first time. The key factor in the electrochemical methods is the choice of suitable electrode materials. For this purpose, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped poly(sulfosalicylic acid) (PSSA) was immobilized on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Apart from exhibition strong and stable electrocatalytic response towards estradiol (E2) and progesterone (P4), the proposed sensor was able to distinguish two hormone's oxidation peaks clearly. Under the optimal conditions, for selective determination of E2, good linear relationships were obtained in the range of 0.001–6.0μmolL–1, with detection limit of 0.23nmolL–1, and for P4 in the range of 0.001–6.0μmolL–1, with the detection limit of 0.31nmolL–1. The prepared sensor possessed accurate and rapid response toward E2 and P4 with an improved stability, selectivity and repeatability. More importantly, the facile and environment-friendly electrochemical construction strategy provided here, may be open a cost-effective way for setting up nanocomposites or nanohybrid-based sensing platform, which extend the application of electrochemical sensor for the green, facile and sensitive analysis of electroactive compounds in biological systems and pharmaceutical formulations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Conducting polymer
Electrochemical synthesis
Estradiol
Progesterone

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterizations of GQDs-PSSA/GO modified electrode
Electrochemical behavior of E2 and P4at the modified electrode
Effect of pH value of supporting electrolyte
Effect of varying the accumulation time and potential
Individual determination of E2 and P4 using GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE
Simultaneous determination of E2 and P4 on GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE
Reproducibility, repeatability, response time and stability studies
Selective determination of E2 and P4 in the presence of interferents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis spectra of CA and GQDs, (B) FT–IR spectra of CA and GQDs, (C) TEM image of GQDs, (D) FT–IR spectra of SSA (a) and PSSA (b), FE–SEM images of (E) PSSA and (F) GQDs-PSSA.

The influence of sulfosalicylic acid monomer concentration (A), percent of GQDs employed in the synthesis of PSSA (B), cycles of electro-polymerization (C), and scan rate of electro-polymerization (D) on the peak currents of E2 and P4 at GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE in a 0.1molL–1 CBS containing 300μmolL–1 of E2 and P4.
CVs of 5.0mmolL–1 [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– in 0.1molL–1 KCl obtained at a scan rate of 100mVs–1. Curve a: bare GCE, curve b: GO/GCE, curve c: PSSA/GO/GCE, curve d: GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE.

CVs of 500μmolL–1 E2 and P4 in CBS of pH 6.0 obtained at (a) bare GCE, (b) GO/GCE, (c) PSSA/GO/GCE and (d) GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE. Inset corresponds to the CV curve of GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE in pH 6.0 CBS; scan rate, 100mVs–1.
(A) Effect of pH on the oxidation of E2 and P4 (300μmolL–1), (B) Plots of I p vs. pH and (C) Plots of E p vs. pH.

(A) CVs of 200μmolL–1 E2 and P4 on GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE in CBS (pH = 6.0) at different scan rates ranging from 10 to 120mVs–1. (B) The linear relationship of the peak current of E2 and P4 vs. scan rate, (C) Variation of the anodic peak potential of E2 and P4 vs. the logarithm of scan rate.

(A) DPVs of E2 with concentrations ranging from 0.001 to 6.5μmolL–1, in the presence P4 of 0.5μmolL–1, in CBS (pH = 6.0), (B) DPVs of P4 with concentrations ranging from 0.8nmolL–1 to 6.0 μmolL–1, in the presence E2 of 0.1μmolL–1, in CBS pH = 6.0. (C) Different concentrations of E2 and P4 in the range of 0.001–6.0μmolL–1 in pH 6.0 CBS. Insets show the linear relationship of the peak currents against concentration of the analytes. Error bar: standard deviation for n = 3.

(A) Continuous DPV sweep in CBS (pH 6.0) containing 0.7μmolL–1 of E2 and 0.1μmolL–1 of P4, (B) DPVs obtained for human serum sample in CBS (pH 6.0) before and after spiking with different concentrations of E2 and P4.
The proposed mechanisms of the electrochemical oxidation of estradiol (A) and progesterone (B).
Characteristics of the analytical performance for simultaneous determination of E2 and P4.
Comparison of the efficiency of some modified electrodes used in the individual determination of E2 and P4.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: rGO: Reduced graphene oxide; DHP: Dihexadecyl phosphate film; CuTthP: Cu(II)-meso-tetrathien-2-ylporphyrin; mAb: Monoclonal antibody; SPCE: Screen Printed Electrode; BiFE: Bismuth film-plated electrode.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of E2 and P4 determination in serum samples by DPV compared with ECL method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of E2 and P4 determination in tablet sample by DPV.

head.ce:title: Analytical methodology for the electro-catalytic determination of estradiol and progesterone based on graphene quantum dots and poly(sulfosalicylic acid) co-modified electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan, Namjoo Street, P.O. Box: 1914, Rasht, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Guilan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The goal of this study was to develop an electroanalytical method for the simultaneous determination of steroid hormones for the first time. The key factor in the electrochemical methods is the choice of suitable electrode materials. For this purpose, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) doped poly(sulfosalicylic acid) (PSSA) was immobilized on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Apart from exhibition strong and stable electrocatalytic response towards estradiol (E2) and progesterone (P4), the proposed sensor was able to distinguish two hormone's oxidation peaks clearly. Under the optimal conditions, for selective determination of E2, good linear relationships were obtained in the range of 0.001–6.0μmolL–1, with detection limit of 0.23nmolL–1, and for P4 in the range of 0.001–6.0μmolL–1, with the detection limit of 0.31nmolL–1. The prepared sensor possessed accurate and rapid response toward E2 and P4 with an improved stability, selectivity and repeatability. More importantly, the facile and environment-friendly electrochemical construction strategy provided here, may be open a cost-effective way for setting up nanocomposites or nanohybrid-based sensing platform, which extend the application of electrochemical sensor for the green, facile and sensitive analysis of electroactive compounds in biological systems and pharmaceutical formulations.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last years, there has been a worldwide interest in chemicals that might be disrupting the endocrine system of humans and wildlife. Herein, it is important to establish new techniques concerning the detection of biological compounds. In particular, the growing need for conventional detection tool with high selectivity and sensitivity is at the heart of modern technology in the field of sensors [1]. One of the primary substances that has high endocrine disruptor activity is estradiol (17 β-estradiol) [2]. Estradiol is the essential steroid hormone with estrogenic activity that is naturally present in mammalian [3]. It is an important bioactive substance, involved in reproduction in women, with relevant roles in many intracellular processes and takes part in women's fertility [4]. Herein, its concentration and changes are closely related to human's health status [5].

The quantification of estradiol levels in serum and urine is important in various clinical practices, in the prevention and treatment of related diseases that occur because of hormone dysfunction and breast cancer [6]. Furthermore, the determination of estradiol is also of interest in environmental monitoring because of its high endocrine disrupting potency and the extensive use of therapies with the synthetic hormone in humans and other animals [7]. Estradiol deficiency can cause diseases such as hyper-androgenism, cancer, heart disease, osteoporosis, and menopausal symptoms [8].

Another important bioactive substance, which belongs to a group of steroid hormones, is progesterone. Progesterone (pregn-4-ene-3,20-dione) is an unsaturated α, β ketone [9]. It plays a vital role in the stabilization and maintenance of pregnancy in mammals, acting in the synthetic route of various biologically active steroids [10,11] and participating in the regulation of the menstrual cycle [12]. Also in the pharmaceutical industry, P4 is the active principle of many drugs used in hormone replacement therapies for post-menopausal women [13,14].

Its imbalance in the human organism can cause malformation problems within the reproductive system [9,12]. P4 is secreted by the uterus to prepare it for pregnancy and maintain the pregnancy after conception secretes P4 [15]. Thus, it is very important to know P4 levels in different reproductive systems which is adequate to diagnose an early pregnancy. Moreover, monitoring of progesterone level in plasma or milk has a particular interest in application areas ranging from clinical fertility measurement to pregnancy diagnosis in veterinary and farming practices [16].

In the view of such an environmental and clinical importance of steroids, for the individual quantify of steroid hormones level, such as estradiol and progesterone in urine, serum and amniotic fluid several attempts have been made by HPLC using various detectors (including UV [17,18], fluorescence [19,20] and electrochemical (EC) [21]), GC–MS [22,23], micellar electrokinetic chromatography [24], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) tests [25,26], and radioimmunoassay (RIA) [27,28] techniques so far.

Nevertheless, most of these methods, although highly accurate and possess high sensitivity [29], are time-consuming, require high-cost equipment, sometimes adversely affect the analytical results and often require several sample preparation steps which limited their applications. Moreover, these techniques usually generate waste-containing organic solvents, which makes the procedure more complicated and expensive [29,30]. In the case of immunoassays, for example, it is necessary to obtain a specific antibody for these steroids [31]. However, enzyme-based biosensors are hindered by the thermal and chemical instabilities of the enzyme [32].

From a contrasting point of view, electroanalytical techniques owing to fast response, low expense, operational simplicity, ability to allow the construction of simple and portable devices for fast screening purposes, in-field/on-site monitoring and high sensitivity, have been widely attracts great concern and a variety of modified electrodes have been reported for hormones determination [30].

Nowadays, the tendency in the field of electroanalytical devices, sensors and biosensors is substituting the organic redox mediators with different nanomaterials due to the growing miniaturization of microelectronic devices. Indeed, nanomaterials modified electrodes show effective catalysis for the reaction involving the analyte, fast mass transport and electron transfer kinetics, large active surface area and good control of electrode microenvironment [33].

In recent years, graphene-based nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO), graphene nanoribbons (GNRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have become a hotspot for various fields of research and shown a grand potential as enhanced materials to fabricate the electrochemical sensing interface, owing to the outstanding electronic, thermal and mechanical properties and good chemical stability [34,35]. In this work, an emerging class of the nano carbon family, ultrafine graphene quantum dots are proposed. GQDs are graphene fragments with lateral dimensions less than 100nm [36–38] which clearly possess a graphene structure inside the dots. GQDs are superior to common carbon materials thanks to its low toxicity, easy preparation, high chemical stability, environmental friendliness, high solubility in many solvents and the possibility of functionalization at their edges [39]. It has shown great promising applications in the fields of sensors, bioanalysis, optical dyes and biological medicine [40]. Furthermore, GQDs expand the contact area with the analyte, which increases the electrochemical effective surface area to interact with some electroactive analytes. Since geometric surface area is a very important parameter in electrochemistry, modification of different substrates by GQDs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction [41]. Therefore, attention has to be paid to a platform for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors by using GQDs in order to obtain an efficient electron transport between the analyte and the surface of conventional electrodes.

Graphite oxide (GO), a two dimensional carbon material, has attracted considerable attention due to its importance in nano-science. GO is easily obtained by the harsh oxidative treatment of graphite powder. After oxidation process, a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups are generated [42]. Moreover, the oxidation process often breaks the π-conjugation, and exfoliates or expands the graphene layers. Thus, the resulting GO product is water dispersible. Because of the functional groups present in GO, the sorption and intercalation of ions and molecules are possible. This feature, together with high specific surface area and easy dispersion, makes GO promising materials to construct novel electrochemical sensing film and to use widely as the supports [42,43].

Since the discovery of the conductance in conjugated polymers [44], these materials have come to the forefront of researches such as battery electrodes, corrosion protectives, and sensors [45] because of their potential application, good stability, good electrical conductivity, and easy preparation [44,46]. Moreover, the modification of electrodes with alternating deposition of functional compounds is a very simple way to experimentally produce complex layered structures with precise control of layer composition and thickness. It is established that thin layers of conducting polymers (CPs) on the surface of substrate electrodes show an improvement of response for the determination of various important biological and clinical species and be able to enhance the kinetics of various electrode processes [47]. CPs have therefore been considered to be useful matrices for the immobilization of dispersed nanomaterials. Thus porous structure of conducting polymer nanocomposite was achieved, which may provide sufficient active sites for the adsorption of target analyte and enhanced charge transport properties compared to the conventionally synthesized CPs [48].

Among the different CPs, poly(sulfosalicylic acid) known as one of the most stable and successful commercially available CPs today. PSSA is ideal for improving the chemo/biosensors materials’ sensitivity because of its easy preparation, good environmental and thermal stability, low cost of starting or raw materials, operation at room temperature and friendly biocompatibility [47]. Moreover, PSSA provides better electrical conductivity [44] and more active sites than the modified electrodes fabricated through covalent bonding or adsorption [49]. Recent developments of facile as well as effective method of synthesis of PSSA nanocomposite with high aspect ratio and increased surface area have generated tremendous interest as sensors. Superior properties of GQDs and the advantages of PSSA have received more attention for preparation of high performance CP/QDs hybrid materials. When GQDs fill the polymer matrix results in conductive polymer nanocomposite. Moreover, the incorporation of GQDs with the sufficient oxygen based functionalities led to significant morphological changes in PSSA layer that improves the carrier conductance [50]. In this context, the synergistic contribution from PSSA and GQDs makes the new material, a capable candidate to prepare of high performance modified electrode materials for the determination of trace levels of biological compounds.

As compared to other methods, electrochemical deposition is a versatile technique for producing surface coatings, owing to its precise controllability, room temperature operation, rapid deposition rate, strong adherence to electrode surface, no need of vacuum, no wastage of chemicals and relatively low cost [51,52]. As far as we know, there have been very few reports about PSSA film-modified glassy carbon electrode.

Hitherto, simultaneous determination of estradiol and progesterone was performed by HPLC [53], SPE/LC-(ESI) MS-MS [54] and LC-MS/MS [55] methods. To the best of our knowledge, no electroanalytical technique was found in the literatures for simultaneous quantitative determination of these hormones.

This paper reports on the novel strategy based on the proposed sensor for the simultaneous electrochemical determination of E2 and P4. Because of the mentioned characteristics of GQDs and CPs, we considered GQDs doped PSSA that would possess large electroactive surface area, better conductivity and more active sites for electrochemical sensing. There are three main objectives in the following sections. The first was to investigate the performance of GQDs-PSSA/GO modified GC electrodes for the electrochemical oxidation of E2 and P4; the second was optimizing the experimental parameters to improve the method efficiency during the experiment; and the third and last aim was to demonstrate the practical application of the present modified electrode by simultaneously determining of E2 and P4 content in clinical and combined pharmaceutical formulations.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out on a Autolab PGSTAT 30 electrochemical analyzer (Ecochemie BV, Utrecht, the Netherlands) controlled by Nova 1.11 software, with a conventional three-electrode system. The modified GCE was used as the working electrode, while the platinum wire served as the auxiliary electrode and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode acted as the reference electrode. GCE was purchased from Azar Electrode Company (Urmia, Iran). Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) optimum parameters were as follows: pulse amplitude, 0.025V; pulse interval time, 0.5s; scan rate, 0.01Vs−1 over the potential range of 0.3–1.1V vs. Ag/AgCl. All the pH values were measured with a Metrohm pH meter (model 827, Swiss made). Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE–SEM; Sigma, Zeiss, Germany), transmission electron microscope (TEM; EM10C–100kV, Zeiss, Germany), and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT–IR; Shimadzu 8900, Kyoto, Japan) were used to characterize the morphology, size and structure of the nanomaterials. UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded at room temperature on Biochrom WPA Biowave II UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The amperometry analysis was carried out by PalmSens analyzer under continuous stirring. Electro-chemiluminescence (ECL) method (Roche, Cobas E411) was used as a reference method for E2 and P4 determination. All measurements were performed at the ambient temperature of the laboratory (ca. 23±1°C).

All chemicals commercially available were of analytical grade from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and were used without further purification. 5-sulfosalicylic acid, graphite powder, potassium chlorate (KClO3) and other reagents were purchased from Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany). Progesterone, estradiol and other hormones were from Aburaihan Pharmaceutical Co. (Tehran, Iran). Citric acid (CA) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Aqueous solutions were prepared with citrate buffer solution (CBS, 0.1molL–1) in the pH range 3.0–6.5 by mixing solutions of 0.1molL–1 citric acid and 0.1molL–1 sodium citrate. The stock solutions of E2 and P4 were prepared fresh at the time of experiments. All solutions were prepared and diluted using high quality deionized water.

GO was synthesized from pure graphite powder via the modified Brodie method [56]. In brief, pure graphite (1.0g) and potassium chlorate (8.0g) were mixed in fuming nitric acid (20mL) at room temperature without subsequent aging and stirred for 24h. The following processes, such as washing, filtration, and cleaning were carried out as in the Brodie method.

The GQDs were synthesized by directly pyrolyzing CA as described elsewhere [57]. In a typical procedure of GQDs preparation, 2.0g CA was put into a 5mL beaker and heated to 200°C for about 30min, until the CA changed to an orange liquid, implying the formation of GQDs. The obtained orange liquid for preparing GQDs was added drop by drop into 100mL of 10mgmL–1 NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH, the aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained and stored in a refrigerator.

Prior to modification, the bare glassy carbon electrode was sequentially polished with 0.05µm Al2O3 slurry on the tracing paper to obtain a mirror-like surface, followed by washing ultrasonically with ethanol and doubly distilled water for 5min. Subsequently, the GCE was cast-coated with 5.0μL of 2.5mgmL−1 GO solution in dimethylformamide and dried in air.

We can electro-polymerize functional monomers on the surfaces of graphite and its derivatives for improved application performance and stability. Thus the GO/GCE was immersed in the aqueous medium which prepared by dissolving 10mmolL–1 sulfosalicylic acid in PBS of pH 5.5 containing synthesized solution of GQDs (15% v/v). The GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE was formed in the prepared mixture solution by cyclic sweeping with a potential scanning range of –1.0–2.0V at 75mVs–1 for 8 scans. The modified electrode was cleaned by CV from –0.5 to +1.0V at 100mVs–1 in PBS (pH 7.0) for removing of the physically adsorbed materials and unpolymerized monomers. For comparison, the PSSA/GO/GCE was prepared at the same mixture solution as mentioned above without adding GQDs. All resulting electrodes were washed with water and stored at room temperature when not in measurements.

In order to evaluate the applicability of the method, the modified electrode was examined to simultaneous determination of estradiol and progesterone in practical samples. Human blood serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers at the Danesh Pathobiology Laboratory (Iran) and stored in a refrigerator before using. The known volume of each fresh blood serum sample (200μL) was diluted 50-fold with pH 6.0 CBS. The resulting solutions were then transferred into the electrochemical cell for subsequent analysis of E2 and P4 without any further pretreatment by a standard addition method using the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE.

Tablet sample was obtained from a local drugstore. For analysis five tablets were accurately weighed and homogenized together by grinding to represent an average and fine powder. A portion of the powder, equivalent amount to one tablet was transferred to a 50mL volumetric flask and dissolved in the mixture solution of ethanol-deionized water (1:1 v/v) and sonicated during 20min at ambient temperature. The solution was subsequently filtered through Whatman 42 (125mm diameter) filter paper to obtain a clear filtrate. Afterwards, 100μL of the solution was attenuated with CBS (pH 6.0) and then was transferred into the electrochemical cell to determine E2 and P4 by DPV method.

The GQDs formation has been confirmed via UV–Vis absorption spectra, FT–IR spectroscopic studies and TEM measurements ( Fig. 1). Fig. 1A presents the UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and CA. According to the UV–Vis results in Ref [57]., the aqueous suspension of GQDs shows absorption band at about 380nm which is not observed in the UV–Vis spectrum of CA.

FT–IR was used to characterize the CA and the obtained GQDs (Fig. 1B). The GQDs exhibit absorption of carboxyl group and hydroxyl group, showing that this nanomaterial contains –COOH groups. Additionally, the CA shows absorption of stretching vibration C–H at 2950cm–1 and stretching vibration of C–H in the range of below 1350cm–1. Contrarily, the GQDs exhibit nearly no absorption of C–H, implying that the CA sample has been carbonized completely. It should be noted that no characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compound (stretching vibration of C–H in aromatic rings around 3000–3100cm–1 and CH bending vibrations at 859, 788, and 728cm–1) are included in these FT–IR spectra, suggesting that no any aromatic compound is contained in the obtained GQDs. As shown in the FT–IR spectrum (Fig. 1B), the spectrum of the obtained GQDs exhibits the bands at 1680cm–1 (C=O)), 1380cm–1 (C–H), 1590cm–1 (C=C)), 1090cm–1 (C–O group stretching vibration) and abroad band around 3400cm–1 (–OH) due to intercalated water molecules present on GQDs [57,58].

The TEM image of the synthesized GQDs is given in Fig. 1C. According to the mentioned Fig., it can be observed that nearly homogeneous nanodisks with the size of GQDs ranging from 10 to 20nm are found. Furthermore, the transparency of the GQDs demonstrates that most of the GQDs consisted of few graphene layers.

The FT–IR spectra (Fig. 1D) illustrated the differences between the fine curves of sulfosalicylic acid (a) and disappears curve of PSSA (b), which is attributed to the poly reaction of sulfosalicylic acid. The spectrum of the sulfosalicylic acid (curve (a)) displays a broad peak around 3405cm–1 which shows O–H stretching. The absorption at about 3000cm–1 in curve (a) is the C–H stretching vibration peak. As depicted in curve (b), the peaks of S–O stretching vibration (1383cm−1 and 1162cm−1), –COO− stretching vibration (1634cm−1 and 1433cm−1) and O–H stretching vibration (3447cm–1) indicating that the monomer have been formed PSSA polymer [47].

As shown in Fig. 1E, the surface morphology of the modified electrodes was analyzed by FE–SEM due to the physical morphology affected on the response of an electrochemical sensor. The typical morphology of PSSA film displayed smooth and homogeneous film which verified that the monomer was successfully polymerized and the GCE surface being covered by PSSA.

As can be seen from Fig. 1F, the FE–SEM revealed wrinkled texture and rough surface at GQDs-PSSA/GO/GC electrode that is associated with the higher surface density of the GQDs-PSSA/GO modified electrode than the PSSA/GO modified electrode. Therefore, the surface area of GCE was increased when the electro-polymerization of sulfosalicylic acid was performed in the presence of GQDs which may enhance the interaction between the modified electrode and the analytes.

All necessary factors influencing the current responses of estradiol and progesterone were carefully studied to explore the best conditions at which the best analytical performance was achieved. To find best performance of GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE, preparation of electrode was optimized by different electro-polymerization conditions to obtain modified GCE.

First, the concentration of sulfosalicylic acid monomer used for the modification of the GO/GCE was studied across a range of 5.0–20.0mmolL–1. The results are shown in Fig. 2A. It was found that the anodic peak heights of E2 and P4 increased when higher concentrations of sulfosalicylic acid monomer were used to construction of the modified electrode, because PSSA is a conducting polymer. The optimum concentration of sulfosalicylic acid monomer was therefore 15.0mmolL–1. Next, the effect of the percent of GQDs was investigated. Mixtures, in which the final concentration of sulfosalicylic acid monomer was held at 15.0mmolL–1, were varied with synthesized GQDs and sulfosalicylic acid solution ratios of 10%, 15%, 20% and 25% v/v for modification. The voltammograms of 300.0 μmol L–1 E2 and P4 were recorded using the modified GCEs. The resultant anodic peak heights are shown in inset of Fig. 2B. The obtained peak height increased when the volume of GQDs increased up to 15% v/v of GQDs to sulfosalicylic acid monomer solution due to the increment of electrode's surface area by GQDs. However, when using higher volume ratio, GQDs can partially coagulate increasing the surface area. However, too much volume ratio of GQDs may lead to undesirable mechanical properties of the electrode, which corresponding decreased the electron transfer process. The solution containing 15% v/v GQDs to sulfosalicylic acid monomer solution was chosen as the suitable electro-polymerization solution.

In order to optimize the other electro-polymerization conditions, the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE was prepared at different scan rates (from 25 to 125mVs–1) and cycle (from 5 to 15) by immersing the GO/GCE into an aqueous solution sulfosalicylic acid (10.0mmolL–1) containing 1.5mL GQDs solution through the cyclic sweeping of the potential from −1.0 to 2.0V. The results (Fig. 2C) showed when the cyclic scan laps increased from 5 to 8, the as-prepared GQDs-PSSA film thickened gradually, and the corresponding peak currents also increased, which could be assigned to the improvement of active sites. However, when the cycle number increased further (e.g. 10, 12, or 15 laps), the peak current value decreased obviously. It may be associated with the thick GQDs–PSSA layer that it hindered the mass transport process of both E2 and P4 and the small number of recognition sites formed at the surface of the modified electrode. Based on the above discussion, the electro-polymerization cycles of 8 were selected.

As shown in Fig. 2D, the oxidation current of E2 and P4 on GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE gradually increase from 25 to 75mVs–1 scan rate of electro-polymerization due to increasing the recognition sites on the electrode surface for the analytes. On the other hand, a further increase in the scan rate, leads to a decrease in the electrode response. This can be attributed to the formation of thicker films, which may render the mass transport difficult and decrease in electrode conductivity. Consequently, the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE was prepared for 8 cycles with a scan rate of 75mVs–1 for throughout this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterizations of GQDs-PSSA/GO modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the interface properties of the modified electrodes, electrochemical behaviors of the step by step surface modification of the working electrode in 5.0mmolL–1 Fe(CN)6 3−/4− solution containing 0.1molL–1 KCl in potential range of −0.4–0.8V with the scan rate of 100mVs−1 are shown in Fig. 3. At the bare GCE (curve a) the cyclic voltammogram of [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– exhibited a poor electrochemical behavior suggesting sluggish electron transfer at the interface.

A comparison among the slowest electron transferring bare GCE, the GO/GCE (curve b), the PSSA/GO/GCE (curve c) and the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE (curve d) showed that the peak current at the nanocomposite electrode was much higher than those at the other electrodes, which proved the role of GQDs. We deduced from well-defined redox pair that the surface properties of the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE were markedly improved as well as the surface area owning to the increased conductivity of electrode in the presence of nanocomposite, because the electron transfers of Fe(CN)6 3–/4– redox couple as electrochemical markers were facilitate downing to the synergistic effect of GQDs and PSSA.

In order to confirm this, since Randles-Sevcik equation [59] implies that peak current of electroactive species is related to electroactive surface area, effective area of modified GCEs were estimated using this equation:
(1)
I
p
= ( 2.69 ×
10
5
) A
D
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
υ
1 / 2
C
where A represents the area of the electrode (cm2), n is the number of electrons participating in the reaction (n = 1), D is the diffusion coefficient of the molecule in solution (6.7 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), C is the concentration (5.0mmolL–1), and υ is the scan rate (Vs−1). The electroactive surface area values of the bare GCE, GO/GCE, PSSA/GO/GCE and GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE were calculated as 0.032, 0.051, 0.131 and 0.224cm2, respectively. The larger reaction area of the latter electrode and the enhanced electron transfer kinetic at the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE might be attributable in part to the high electrical conductivity and synergistic effect between PSSA and GQDs in nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of E2 and P4at the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In order to discern the role and electro-catalytic activity of different working electrodes, the voltammetric data of analyte mixture in CBS (pH 6.0) recorded at the modified electrodes and bare GCE ( Fig. 4). There was no oxidation peak observed in the absence of E2 and P4 at GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE (Fig. 4, inset), suggesting that the GQDs–PSSA/GO/GCE was non-electroactive in the potential range of 0.2–1.3V in CBS (pH 6.0). When two analytes were added into CBS, the modified electrode gave two obviously oxidation peaks for estradiol and progesterone (500μmolL–1), respectively. It could be seen in Fig. 4 that at the unmodified GCE (curve a), the voltammogram of E2 and P4 oxidation exhibited just two poor peaks. At the GO/GCE (curve b) and the PSSA/GO/GCE (curve c) two peaks were exhibited, indicating that the oxidation peaks of E2 and P4 could be separated, but the peak currents were rather small at the surface of mentioned electrodes indicating a slow electron transfer kinetic. In contrast, under the identical conditions, the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE (curve d) exhibits remarkably different voltammetric behavior with significant improvement of peak currents and the sharpness of the peaks together with substantial negative shift in the oxidation peak potentials. In the reverse scan no reduction is observable (also not in the negative potential range; not shown), which revealed completely irreversible oxidation of E2 and P4. Meanwhile, it could be noticed that the magnitudes of E2 and P4 electro-oxidation signal (peak currents) were found to be about 8 and 11 times relative to the unmodified electrode, respectively. These remarkable improvements in electrochemical sensitivity towards E2 and P4 could be ascribed to the several major factors: (1) GQDs provide more active sites and superior electrical conductivity which would accelerate the facile electron transfer between the electrode and species in solution; (2) poly(sulfosalicylic acid) itself has good catalytic ability and improved mass transport; (3) The as-prepared GQDs doped PSSA/GO composite owned promoted kinetics of the electrochemical process and higher effective surface areas which would facilitate the accumulation of the hormone molecules at the surface of electrode, probably originating from the synergistic effect and excellent cooperation of GQDs and PSSA.

Moreover, the peak-to-peak separation of E2 and P4 was 300mV. The large peak-to-peak separation could effectively decrease the interaction between progesterone and estradiol in simultaneous determination and increase the determination selectivity.

The type of supporting electrolyte played a key role in the electrochemical oxidation behaviors of 300 μmol L–1 E2 and P4. Different supporting electrolytes including phosphate buffer, acetate buffer, citrate-phosphate buffer and citrate buffer were used to estimate the current response of E2 and P4 at GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE. The excellent oxidation response was obtained in citrate buffer because the peak current was the highest and the shape of peak was better (not shown). As a result, citrate buffer was chosen as the determining medium for E2 and P4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH value of supporting electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 illustrates the effect of pH on the response of the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE in electrochemical oxidation of E2 and P4 using LSV technique. The dependence of the peak current (I p) and of the peak potential (E p) on the pH over the range of 3.0–6.5 in 0.1molL–1 CBS was investigated. The well-resolved oxidation peaks with low background levels and maximum oxidation currents of the electrochemical reaction of E2 and P4 were observed at pH = 6.0 (Fig. 5A). As shown in Fig. 5B, by increasing the pH from 3.0 to 6.5, the oxidation responses increase and further increasing of the pH leads to the decreasing of the oxidation currents. Therefore, pH = 6.0, which showed the highest peak currents and by considering the physiological condition was chosen as the optimal pH value for further analytical experiments.

In addition, due to the association of protons transfer process in the electrode reaction, the electro-oxidation peak potentials of the analytes shifted to the negative direction when the pH of supporting electrolyte was increased (Fig. 5C). Good linear relationship was established between the oxidation peak potential (E pa) of E2 and P4 and the solution pH with: E E2 = −0.0689pH + 1.0781 (R 2 = 0.993) and E P4 = −0.0707pH + 1.3509 (R 2 = 0.993), respectively. According to Nernst equation [60], the formula:
(2)
d
E
pa
/
d
pH
=
(
− 0 . 059
m
/
n
)
where m is the number of proton participating in the electrochemical reaction and n is the number of electrons. The slope of dE pa/dpH plots for E2 was obtained −0.0689VpH–1, which is close to the theoretical value (–0.0590VpH–1) expected from the Nernst equation and it is suggested that an electrochemical process accomplished by the transfer of same number of protons and electrons during the electro-oxidation of estradiol. This result is in agreement with the work developed by Moraes et al. [30]. In the case of P4, the obtained slope of −0.0707VpH–1 was almost similar to that shown in previous electrochemical investigations and it implied that in the electrode reaction process follows a mechanism involving equal number of proton and electron [29].

In order to clarify the oxidation process and to obtain the kinetic parameters, the scan rate dependence of electro-oxidation responses was investigated by employing cyclic voltammetry (CV) in different scan rates ranging from 10 to 120mVs–1 in pH 6.0 CBS containing 200μmolL–1 E2 and P4 ( Fig. 6A). The plot of I pa as a function of ν for two molecules was also shown in the Fig. 6B. As can be seen in this Fig., with the increase in the scan rate the anodic peak currents of P4 and E2 go on increase continuously. Plotting the I pa against v established linear regression equations within the range of 10–120mVs–1, as can be described by the below equations:
(3)
I
pa
= 194.69 v + 7.183
(
R ² = 0.992
)
for
E2
(4)
I
pa
= 140.93 v + 6.199
(
R ² = 0.995
)
for
P4

The equations verify that the system presents feature corresponding to a surface bound controlled process for the electro-oxidation of these biomolecules on GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE at this range of scan rates.

As it is obvious from Fig. 6A, the catalytic oxidation peak potentials shift slightly to more positive values with increasing scan rate for the two analytes, which confirms the irreversibility of the oxidation process. The relationship of E pa with logν was further constructed (Fig. 6C) for the calculation of the electrochemical parameters which can be expressed as: E pa(V) = 0.1047logν (Vs–1) + 0.7275 (R 2 = 0.994) for E2 and E pa(V) = 0.0941logν (Vs–1) + 0.9829 (R 2 = 0.995) for P4. According to the Laviron theory for an irreversible electrode process, E pa is calculated by the following equation [61]:
(5)
E
p
=
E
0
+
(
RT
α nF
)
ln ⁡ (
RT
k
s
α nF
) −
(
RT
α nF
)
ln
ν
where α is the transfer coefficient, k s is the heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant for the reaction, n is the number of electrons transferred, ν is the scan rate, and E° is the formal redox potential. Other symbols have their usual meanings. The value of αn was calculated easily from the slope of the equation in Fig. 6C, and its αn was 0.57 for E2, and 0.62 for P4, respectively.

In order to find information on the rate determining step, a useful device is the Tafel plot (not shown) which depicted using the data derived from the rising part of a voltammogram E2 and P4 oxidation under quasi-static conditions at scan rate of 0.02Vs−1. This part of voltammogram, known as Tafel region, is affected by electron transfer kinetics between analytes and GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE. The Tafel slope was obtained using the following equation [62]:
(6)
E =
log
I
0
+
[
2.303 R T /
(
1 − α
)
n F
]
log
I
where I 0 and α are the exchange current and the transfer coefficient of the oxidation of analytes, respectively. The slope of this plot was 0.114 and 0.159V decade−1 for E2 and P4 respectively. By substituting αn values for E2 and P4 from Laviron equation in the Tafel slope, the number of electrons involved in the rate-limiting step (n) was obtained to be 1 for both E2 and P4. Because the electroactive group in estradiol molecule is hydroxyl group and involvement of one electron in the oxidation process of estradiol, it can be deduced that the reactant site for the estradiol oxidation is hydroxyl group in aromatic ring [30]. As stated above, the number of protons and electrons taking part in the electrochemical reaction of progesterone is 1:1 and allylic hydrogen can be assumed as the probable reactant site of this molecule. Integrating the above results with pH investigation, it could be concluded that the electro-oxidation of estradiol and progesterone on GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE was a one-electron and one-proton process at rate determining step for both analytes ( Scheme 1).

The values of the charge transfer coefficient (α) of E2 and P4 were calculated by slopes of Tafel plot as 0.48 and 0.60, respectively with the involvement of one electron in the rate determining step of each electrode process. It can be seen that the value of α is quite close to its theoretical value.

These results clearly indicate that doping of GQDs into PSSA layer remarkably increases the electron transfer property as well as the surface area. The surface concentration (Г, molcm–2) of electroactive E2 and P4 on the modified electrode GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE and bare GCE can be estimated from the slope of the plot of I pa versus scan rate (Fig. 6B) by [63]:
(7)
I
p
=
n
2
F
2
ν A Г / 4 R T
where n is the number of electrons transferred, F (Cmol–1) is the Faraday's constant, A (cm2) is the area of the electrode, Г is the surface concentration of the electroactive substances and ν (Vs–1) is the scan rate. Based on the relationship of I p with ν, the surface concentrations on the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE were estimated to be 9.10 × 10–3 μmolcm–2 and 7.90 × 10–4 μmolcm–2 for E2 and P4 respectively, which were larger than the corresponding values 5.70 × 10–4 μmolcm–2 and 2.8 × 10–4 μmolcm–2 on GCE. The obtained results can imply that the existence of GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE providing a large active surface area and promoting the electron transfer between the biomolecules and the electrode surface for both electroactive species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of varying the accumulation time and potential
ce:section.ce:para: In electrochemical determination, accumulation is an effective way to improve the determination sensitivity and efficiency. In this work, given the fact that E2 and P4 were able to be adsorbed at the surface of the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE the effect of accumulation time and accumulation potential on the oxidation peak currents of 100μmolL–1 E2 and P4 were investigated at pH 6.0 under stirring. At the end of the accumulation period the stirrer was stopped and the solution was allowed to become quiescent for 15s prior of the voltammetric scan. The results (data not shown) indicated that the peak currents of both E2 and P4 increased with accumulation time growing from 0 to 300s at the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE, but after 150 and 180s of accumulation time for E2 and P4, respectively they kept almost unchanged, meaning that the adsorption quantity of analytes at the electrode surface reached saturation and the peak height become constant. Therefore, an accumulation time of 180s was chosen as the optimal time for further studies.

In order to evaluate the influence of accumulation potential on the determination of E2 and P4, the oxidation peak currents of 100μmolL–1 E2 and P4 after 180s accumulation under different accumulation potentials ranging from −0.2 to 0.4V were measured by DPV. The results (data not shown) revealed that the oxidation peak currents were independent of accumulation potential, and the maximum peak currents were almost equal to those observed for open-circuit accumulation. Therefore, the subsequent experiments were carried out under the open-circuit conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual determination of E2 and P4 using GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Since DPV has higher current sensitivity than CV and taking into account the ability of this technique to generate sharper and well resolved peaks at lower concentrations, DPV was employed to test the versatility of the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE. For individual determination of E2 or P4, several binary mixtures of the two hormones were prepared, in which the concentration of one hormone changed gradually, whereas the concentration of another hormone was kept constant. DP voltammograms of these solutions were recorded, as shown in Fig. 7.

Fig. 7A depicts the typical DP voltammograms with varied concentrations of E2 and constant concentration of P4 (0.5μmolL–1). The peak current of E2 increased linearly with an increase in its concentration (inset of Fig. 7A) over the first linear dynamic range of 0.001–0.07μmolL–1 and the second was 0.07–6.5 μmol L–1 with the linear functions of I pa(μA) = 65.759 C (μmolL–1) + 0.0472 (R 2 = 0.997) and I pa(μA) = 5.922 C (μmolL–1) + 5.505 (R 2 = 0.997) respectively.

Similarly, as shown in Fig. 7B, the independent determination of P4 was achieved in solution containing E2 at a fixed concentration of 0.1μmolL–1. Anodic peak current increased linearly with increasing concentration of P4. The calibration plot for P4 (inset of Fig. 7B) over the concentration ranges of 0.8nmolL–1 to 0.1μmolL–1 and 0.1–6.0μmolL–1 were fitted to the regression equations of I pa(μA) = 39.074C (μmol L–1) + 0.2608 (R 2 = 0.998) and I pa(μA) = 5.1336 C (μmolL–1) + 4.2227 (R 2 = 0.998), respectively. The voltammetric peak of one species was unaltered and the peak current remains constant where the peak current of another species enhanced with increasing concentration. Therefore, it confirms that GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE can be used for the simultaneous and selective determination of E2 and P4, because the oxidation peaks of these two hormones are clearly independent from each other. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) of the present sensor (3S b/m) for individual determination of E2 and P4 in the presence of another analyte, obtained from linear regions of the calibration curve that were calculated to be 0.21 and 0.31nmolL–1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of E2 and P4 on GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: From a clinical point of view, simultaneous determination of E2 and P4 is of great importance since these two hormones coexist in practical samples. Under the optimal experimental conditions described above, the possibility of using the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE for simultaneous determination of E2 and P4 was verified by the DPV as their concentrations were simultaneously changed in a range from 0.001 to 6.0μmolL–1 E2 and P4 (Fig. 7C). The peak currents increase independently with increasing concentration of each compound, but their peak potentials were unchanged. The corresponding calibration equations, sensitivity, the LOD and limit of quantitation (LOQ) values are presented in Table 1.

The simultaneous determination of these analytes was achieved on the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE with high sensitivity and selectivity. The LOD and LOQ were calculated based on 3S b/m and 10S b/m respectively, where S b is the standard deviation of the blank, and m is the slope of the regression line of the calibration curve.

The low detection limit can be attributed to the effect of GQDs-PSSA/GO nanocomposite which provided a large specific surface area to increase the loading amount of both E2 and P4. Therefore, the GQDs-PSSA, as advanced electrode materials, may have promising application in electrochemical detection. Both LOD values and wide linear ranges confirmed the sensitivity of the modified electrode which can be hence recommended for the trace determination of E2 and P4 in the practical samples.

The reason for the phenomenon of two linear regions may be as follows: At higher concentrations, saturation took place and this defined the decrease in the current in the second linear range or analytes transport mechanism altered. Moreover, the difference in the slopes for the calibration curves is probably due to the different activity of the electrode surface with low and high concentration of the analytes. In the lower concentration, due to a high number of active sites (in relation to the total number of the analyte molecules), the slope of the first calibration curve is high. While in the higher concentration of analyte, due to decreasing active sites (in relation to the total number of analyte molecules, mainly at the surface of the electrode), therefore caused a decline in the sensitivity of the second calibration [29].

Compared with other modified sensors listed in Table 2, it can be seen that the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE exhibited a wider linear range and lower detection limit in comparison with other reported results. It should be originating from the electrocatalytic activity of the synthesized nanocomposite of GQDs and PSSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, repeatability, response time and stability studies
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, reproducibility of the electrochemical response was checked, by taking triplicate DPV measurements of the current responses to the mixed sample containing 1.0μmolL–1 E2 and P4 using a series of seven modified electrodes, all prepared by the same fabrication process at different days. The reproducibility expressed in relative standard deviation (RSD) which obtained 2.41% for E2 and 2.68% for P4. This is due to the stable dispersion of GQDs in polymer layer and improved electro-synthesis of nanocomposite.

Furthermore, to characterize the repeatability of the current response of the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE, seven successive determinations of solutions containing 1.0μmolL–1 of E2 and P4 was made in a day. The result of seven repetitive measurements with the same modified electrode displayed that the anodic peak currents were nearly constant with small RSD values of 1.53% and 1.48% for E2 and P4 respectively, which revealed an excellent repeatability. This is due to the stability of the modified electrode and the cleaning step during DPV analysis. Moreover, the excellent repeatability of the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE confirms that the electrode is not poisoned by the oxidation products of estradiol and progesterone and that it is capable of repeated use.

The response time of the sensor which can be obtained by the I–t plots was defined as the time after analyte addition for the sensor's response to reach from 10% to 90% of its final value. The response times of the proposed sensor toward E2 and P4 were achieved from the amperograms 2.2 and 2.4s, respectively.

In order to demonstrate the sensing stability of GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE, the electro-oxidation responses were evaluated by 75th continuous DP voltammetric sweep in CBS containing 0.7μmolL–1 of E2 and 0.1μmolL–1 of P4. The results showed that the peak currents dropped to 95% and 94% of the initial values for E2 and P4 respectively, after 75 continuous cycles ( Fig. 8A), which suggested the excellent stability of GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE for their simultaneous detection, continuously.

The lifetime of the developed electrochemical sensor is of particular concern, as the nanocomposite coating may wear off during the stirring and cleaning period. Thus the long-term stability of the as-prepared modified electrode was also verified over a 35-day period using the same electrode coating to detect the same E2 and P4 concentration. The analytical performances did not show an obvious decline. Both current signal changes of the peak currents of E2 and P4 were less than 7% at 7th week, demonstrating that the electrode had satisfactory stability in the air over unused time. These results indicate that the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE has a good reproducibility, stability and does not undergo surface fouling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective determination of E2 and P4 in the presence of interferents
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference ability and selectivity of GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE was investigated by detecting the responses of the modified electrode to E2 and P4 (0.7μmolL–1) in the presence of possible interfering species that can be found commonly with the target analytes in pharmaceuticals or in biological fluids. If the tested coexisting interferents brought the detection current signal deviation below 10% under sufferable coexisting amount, we considered that the foreign substances have no interference.

Ions containing K+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, NH4 +, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Zn2+ and Cl− did not also interfere with electrochemical responses of desired analytes (until >2000 folds). This implies that the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE can be selectively used in real samples which contain common ions or other species. Furthermore, there are some important compounds such as organic acids, amino acids, hormones, sugars which may coexist with E2 and P4 in real samples. It is found that no interference could be observed for glucose (CInterference/CAnalyte = n, n = 1000) testosterone and dopamine (n = 300), hydrocortisone (n = 350), uric acid, ascorbic acid, folic acid (n = 500) and alanine, cysteine, methionine, L-tryptophan, glycine, leucine, arginine (n = 500), in the analysis of target analytes. The obtained data suggesting that the proposed electrode has interference-free signals for the measurement of these two types of analytes in body fluids and pharmaceutical formulations. Nevertheless, the GQDs-PSSA/GO/GCE is sufficiently selective for simultaneous detection of E2 and P4 in real samples.

In an attempt to verify the feasibility and validity of present sensor for analysis of E2 and P4 in real samples, measurements were performed by the standard addition method. More detail processes of preparation were described in experimental section. DPV curves were recorded between 0.4 and 1.0V after 180s accumulation under open-circuit. No additional oxidation peaks from potentially interfering compounds such as amino acids, glucose and other biological substances which may be present in the serum samples occurred in the potential range of E2 and P4 oxidation. Table 3 shows the content of E2 and P4 in the two women serum samples, who were in normal (serum 1) and pregnancy (serum 2) conditions. As shown in Fig. 8, the human serum sample 1 gave two well-defined peaks around 0.55 and 0.81V. In order to ascertain that these peaks are due to the oxidation of E2 and P4, the human serum sample was spiked with standard solutions of E2 and P4. It can be clearly seen that the currents of observed peaks increased with spiking were then detected using this sensor, thereby confirming that two peaks corresponded to the oxidation of E2 and P4, respectively.

Furthermore, the results for the determination of E2 and P4 in human serum samples obtained by this sensor was compared with the reference values that obtained by ECL method using Student's t-test and variance ratio F-test (Table 3). It can be seen that no significant difference exists between the performances of the two methods because the t values for both E2 and P4 in these human serum samples are below the theoretical value of t (2.78, P = 0.95), and the F values of them are below 39.00 (P = 0.95). Therefore, the results from this sensor and ECL are in good agreement, indicative of good accuracy.

E2 and P4 contents of tablet sample were calculated by using standard addition method. Results obtained by DPV technique are in good agreement with the declared E2 and P4 content. The recovery test was obtained for the voltammetric signals to confirm the accuracy of this method and the observed results were shown in Table 4. It can be also observed that the sensor gives recoveries of 99.0% and 101.6% for E2 and P4, respectively, suggesting good accuracy of the prepared sensor in the analysis of real samples. The RSD of 1.04% (E2) and 0.49% (P4) confirmed that the results are reproducible for assay of analytes simultaneously in pharmaceutical samples.

This indicates that the matrix effect did not have a significant role on the performance of the electrochemical sensor. Thus, the proposed sensor is promising for the in-vivo and in-vitro determination of a binary mixture of E2 and P4 in biologically relevant samples.

Utilizing the unique properties of GQDs, such as their high specific surface area and subtle electronic properties, a GQDs-based sensor was used for the biological compounds determination. In this study, GQDs-PSSA nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by a facile and green method, which can be used as an ideal platform to simultaneously detect E2 and P4 using DPV technique for the first time. Further investigation should be performed to increase the lifetime of the electrode and to optimize the performance of the electrochemical sensor. Compared with the GO/GCE and PSSA/GO/GCE, the GQDs-PSSA/GO nanocomposite/GCE exhibited higher electrocatalytic activity toward oxidation of E2 and P4 and higher oxidation peak current attributable to superior conductivity and better site accessible surface for fast recognition. Therefore, the synergistic contribution of PSSA and GQDs makes a new electrode material which shows precise responses, excellent reproducibility and long-term stability, in addition to providing good recovery for E2 and P4 in human serum samples and pharmaceutical formulation. Thus, this novel nanocomposite promises to be a good platform for efficient electrochemical sensors. The suggested sensor permits work at very low concentrations with minimal costs and potential for in-situ monitoring, particularly for point-of-care applications where simplicity and the availability of electrode materials must also be considered. Moreover, it could be a good alternative to conventional methods, which for the determination of analyte species, need too expensive and complex equipment. The interfering effect of biological compounds can be eliminated by the proposed sensor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the post-graduate office of Guilan University (GU1395/21FC) for the support of this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Point-of-care testing (POCT): current techniques and future perspectives

Direct electron transfer of glucose oxidase at glassy carbon electrode modified with functionalized carbon nanotubes within a dihexadecyl phosphate film

Electrochemical determination of estradiol using a thin film containing reduced graphene oxide and dihexadecylphosphate

Determination of progesterone in commercial formulations and in non-conventional micellar systems
Molecularly imprinted hydrogels from colloidal crystals for the detection of progesterone
Studies about the adsorption of progesterone (P4) at glassy carbon electrodes in aqueous buffer solution by square wave voltammetry
Drug therapy: Hormonal treatment of postmenopausal women
Progestogens in hormonal replacement therapy: new molecules, risks, and benefits
Detection of progesterone in whole blood samples
An electrochemical immunosensor for milk progesterone using a continuous flow system
Analysis of steroid hormones in effluents of wastewater treatment plants by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Determination of estrogens in water by HPLC–UV using cloud point extraction
Determination of steroid sex hormones in urine matrix by stir bar sorptive extraction based on monolithic material and liquid chromatography with diode array detection
Development of a quantum dot-based fluorescent immunoassay for progesterone determination in bovine milk
Analysis and occurrence of pharmaceuticals, estrogens, progestogens and polar pesticides in sewage treatment plant effluents, river water and drinking water in the Llobregat river basin
Method based on solid-phase microextraction high-performance liquid chromatography with UV and electrochemical detection to determine estrogenic compounds in water samples
Fast and sensitive liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry assay for seven androgenic and progestagenic steroids in human serum
Determination of steroid estrogens in wastewater by high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry
Enzyme Immunoassays, from Concept to Product Development
Determination of estradiol by biotin-avidin-amplified electrochemical enzyme immunoassay
An electrochemical ELISA procedure for the screening of 17β-estradiol in urban waste waters
Integrated electrochemical immunosensor with gold nanoparticles for the determination of progesterone
Magnetic nanoparticles embedded with graphene quantum dots and multiwalled carbon nanotubes as a sensing platform for electrochemical detection of progesterone
Sensitive determination of 17β–estradiol in river water using a graphene based electrochemical sensor
Electroanalytical determination of estriol hormone using a boron-doped diamond electrode
Electrodeposition of nickel oxide and platinum nanoparticles on electrochemically reduced graphene oxide film as a nonenzymatic glucose sensor
The use of nanoparticles in electroanalysis: an updated review
Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films
Control the size and surface chemistry of graphene for the rising fluorescent materials
Photo-fenton reaction of graphene oxide: a new strategy to prepare graphene quantum dots for DNA cleavage
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Au nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots co-modified glassy carbon electrode for catechol sensing
Graphene quantum dot modified glassy carbon electrode for the determination of doxorubicin hydrochloride in human plasma
Exfoliated graphite oxide modified electrode for the selective determination of picomolar concentration of lead
Improved voltammetric peak separation and sensitivity of uric acid and ascorbic acid at nanoplatelets of graphitic oxide
Fabrication of silver nanoparticles–polypyrrole composite modified electrode for electrocatalytic oxidation of hydrazine
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of poly(3-methylthiophene) and poly(3-octylthiophene) film electrodes
Determination of L-tryptophan in the presence of ascorbic acid and dopamine using poly (sulfosalicylic acid) modified glassy carbon electrode
Template synthesis of conducting polymeric nanocones of poly(3-methylthiophene)
Study on the electrochemical behavior of dopamine with poly(sulfosalicylic acid) modified glassy carbon electrode
Novel and high-performance asymmetric micro-supercapacitors based on graphene quantum dots and polyaniline nanofibers
Simultaneous electrochemical determination of dopamine, ascorbic acid, and uric acid using poly (acid chrome blue K) modified glassy carbon electrode
Redox deposition of nanoscale metal oxides on carbon for next-generation electrochemical capacitors
Development and validation of a liquid chromatographic method for the simultaneous determination of estradiol, estriol, estrone, and progesterone in pharmaceutical preparations
Simultaneous determination of ten endocrine hormone disrupters in water using SPE/LC–(ESI) MS–MS
Simultaneous determination of levonorgestrel and two endogenous sex hormones in human plasma based on LC–MS/MS
On the atomic weight of graphite
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
Electrochemical sensor based on graphene doped gold nanoparticles modified electrode for detection of diethylstilbestrol
General expression of the linear potential sweep voltammogram in the case of diffusionless electrochemical systems
Electrocatalytic oxidation of methanol on glassy carbon electrode modified with nickel-manganese salen complexes encapsulated in mesoporous zeolite A
Electrochemical determination of estradiol using a poly(L-serine) film-modified electrode
Studies towards a disposable screen printed amperometric biosensor for progesterone
